# Questions in Livestream 48

Live Stream #48: Good Friday Orthodox Style.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:23</a> | In Psalm 22:16, should the text read "pierced," "like a lion," or something combining both ideas? | He treats the textual issue as complex and works through the Hebrew evidence rather than giving a simplistic one-word answer. | He explains that the difference between "like a lion" and "pierced" turns on one Hebrew letter, with the Masoretic Text reading "like a lion" but the Septuagint and a Dead Sea Scroll fragment supporting "pierced." He says he is not forcing a messianic conclusion and would accept "like a lion" if the evidence led there, but in this case he thinks the stronger textual evidence favors "pierced." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:36</a> | What is the most likely year and season for Jesus's birth? | He says the year can be narrowed more confidently than the month, while some seasonal proposals remain tentative. | He narrows the year to roughly 4-6 BC and leans toward 4 BC, while stressing that the month cannot be known. He rules out winter because travel in torrential rains and flash-flood conditions was not feasible, also doubts the height of summer because of heat, and says late spring after the barley harvest or early fall are more plausible but still uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=1095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:15</a> | What do you think of the Shroud of Turin and the newer scattering-data arguments for an older date? | He remains unconvinced that it is Christ's shroud. | He argues from John 20 that Jesus's burial cloths are described as multiple linen wrappings plus a separate face cloth, while the Shroud of Turin is a single unified cloth. For him, even a first-century date would not overcome that mismatch with the Gospel description, though he does not criticize people whose faith is encouraged by the object. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=1297" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:37</a> | What does Genesis 2:5 mean, and does it support young-earth creationist claims? | He says the passage needs to be read in context and does not think the common YEC use of it is compelling. | He reads Genesis 2:4-6 as context for the question and says young-earth creationists often treat the lack of shrubs, plants, rain, or human cultivation as proof of a tight creation-week chronology. He thinks that is largely proof by assertion because old-earth creationists and theistic evolutionists also have ways to account for the text, and he says he would need a more specific YEC claim before giving a more detailed answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=1544" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:44</a> | Did Revelation 19:11-14 happen around 66-70 CE, as some preterists argue from Josephus and Tacitus? | He says the proposal does not work for him. | He says the historical fit is weak, especially because Nero died in 68 AD while the temple was destroyed in 70 AD under the events around Vespasian and Titus. He acknowledges that reports of armies or chariots in the sky may be interesting apocalyptic or poetic material, but he does not think they solve the broader historical problems with reading Revelation 19 as fulfilled in the Jewish War. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=1831" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:31</a> | Is there evidence that Jews lived in Egypt in the sixth century BCE, as reflected in Jeremiah 44? | He points to evidence for Israelite/Judahite communities in Egypt and treats Jeremiah's setting as historically plausible. | He identifies Memphis, Pathros, and Elephantine-related areas and points to Aramaic papyri from Israelite mercenaries at Elephantine, along with evidence from Pelusium in the north. He connects Jeremiah's warnings to later trouble at Elephantine, including conflict over the Yahweh temple and evidence that the community adopted practices such as incense and libations to the queen of heaven, while maintaining some correspondence with Judah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=2365" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:25</a> | Do Egyptian daybooks provide a precedent for Jacob's family keeping written records in Avaris or Goshen? | He says Egyptian record-keeping does provide useful background, and he discusses how such documents functioned. | He says no Hyksos daybooks survive, but New Kingdom examples such as Deir el-Medina daybooks show local administrators recording year, month, day, and notable events in an orderly sequence. From that record-keeping culture, he thinks similar records probably existed in larger settlements such as Thebes, Per-Ramesses, Avaris, and Goshen, and he adds that Moses would likely have received scribal training in Egyptian scripts and Akkadian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=2846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:26</a> | How is the temple motif woven into Genesis 1-2, and what should I read on it? | He endorses the temple-reading approach and recommends John Walton as a helpful starting point. | He recommends John Walton as the best starting point and summarizes Genesis 1 as God ordering sacred space and filling its zones, comparable to graded temple spaces. He links the image-and-likeness language to idol terminology, while clarifying that humanity is not a pagan idol, and then treats Eden as the holy place where God meets humanity, later guarded by cherubim and a flaming sword. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=3226" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:46</a> | Did the magicians in Exodus 7 use magic, sleight of hand, or is it myth? | He is open to magic or sleight of hand, rejects myth, and says deriving sleight of hand from "hidden" is an etymological fallacy. | He says Egyptians practiced ritual magic, sleight of hand, religious fictions, and snake handling, so either magic or stage-like technique is possible in Exodus 7. He is firm that he does not regard the episode as myth, but says he cannot decide between magic and sleight of hand without having been there; he also agrees that arguing for sleight of hand from an etymology meaning "hidden" is fallacious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=3465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:45</a> | Did ancient Egypt have an ablution ritual like baptism? | Yes; Egyptian priestly purification included ritual bathing, clean linens, sandals, and natron mouth washing. | He says Egyptian priests purified themselves before temple service by shaving, bathing ritually, putting on clean linen and papyrus sandals, and washing the mouth with natron. He then compares this to Israelite priestly purification, which retained ritual washing and changing clothes but not all Egyptian features, and notes that baptism's meaning later broadened in Jewish and Christian contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=3670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:10</a> | Was Noah polygamous, and how would that fit with the eight souls on the ark? | He treats Noah's polygamy as a long-shot possibility, not his preferred view, and says "eight" may not strictly exclude more people. | He says he does not think Noah's polygamy is the best interpretation, but he also does not see First Peter's reference to eight people as an absolute limiter because mentioning eight does not necessarily exclude additional people in every context. He calls the view an out-there, long-shot possibility and adds that the text does not explicitly say Noah's wife was Ham's mother, though he is not presenting that as his own preferred reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=3964" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:04</a> | Did Egypt have a strong naval presence in the Mediterranean and shipbuilding beyond Nile transport? | Egypt had a strong Nile navy and Mediterranean shipping and trade, but not a large Mediterranean military navy. | He distinguishes military naval presence from commercial shipping: Egypt had Nile warships and naval personnel, plus ships trading widely around the Mediterranean, but he does not see evidence for a large Mediterranean war navy. He says the Nile fleet protected vulnerable delta and frontier points such as Per-Ramesses, Avaris, and Tjaru and also policed the Nile because most Egyptian trade moved by boat rather than road. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=4328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:08</a> | Why do Orthodox Bibles have more Old Testament chapters or books, and who removed them? | It depends on which Orthodox canon; differences come from Septuagint and apocryphal traditions and expanded book forms. | He cautions that there is no single Orthodox Bible and points to differences among Oriental Orthodox and Eastern Orthodox traditions, including the much larger Ethiopian/Eritrean canon. He explains that some differences come from using the Septuagint rather than the Masoretic Text, including apocryphal books and expanded forms of some books, so the answer depends on the specific canon being compared. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=4524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:24</a> | Why was Moses worried about being stoned for sacrificing what was an abomination to Egyptians in Exodus 8:26? | Sheep or goat sacrifice would offend Egyptian purity expectations, so Moses gives that as the reason for going away from Egypt. | He says Yahweh sacrifices would ordinarily involve sheep or lambs, which Egyptians regarded as unclean for sacrifice even though cattle sacrifice was acceptable. Since Canaanite temples in Egypt had become Egyptianized by Moses's time, sacrificing a sheep at a temple would provoke a riot, so Moses gives distance from Egypt as the reason for needing to go out three days, whether or not one also sees that as a pretense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=4755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:15</a> | What are your favorite Old and New Testament books? | Exodus for the Old Testament; John for the New Testament. | He says Exodus is his favorite Old Testament book because it is full of Egypt and fits what he loves to study. For the New Testament he chooses John, describing it as highly theological while still Christ-centered, and uses the answer to advise students to specialize in the material they genuinely love reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=5402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:02</a> | What are your thoughts on Torah observance for Messianic believers? | Keep the weightier matters and spirit of Torah; Christ fulfilled the ceremonial and sacrificial burden, so kosher and similar observance is not required. | He distinguishes different uses of "law" and says Christ fulfilled the sacrificial and ceremonial burden, including the temple-sacrifice system and, in his view, kosher obligations. He does not object to Jewish believers continuing inherited practices, but he says Messianic believers should not obsess over minutiae and should focus on the weightier matters: justice, mercy, grace, truth, loving God, and loving neighbor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=5984" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:44</a> | What are your thoughts on the exclusivity of priesthood in Genesis 47:22? | He connects it to Egyptian priestly land endowments that kings generally did not reclaim. | He reads Genesis 47:20-22 in light of Egyptian land endowments granted to major cults, where priestly lands produced offerings that were later redistributed to priests. Although the king technically owned the land, these allotments were usually treated as belonging to the cult in perpetuity; he names Akhenaten's seizure of temple lands as the major exception because it was intended to cut off temple offerings economically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=6411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:51</a> | Was the covenant made with Israel an ancient suzerain-vassal treaty? | Yes; he says Sinai's covenant structure resembles Late Bronze Age suzerain-vassal treaty forms. | He says the Sinai covenant is structured like Late Bronze Age suzerain-vassal treaties, especially those known from Hittite comparisons. He points to features such as stipulations, blessings and curses, divine appeal or oath formulae, and notes that in Yahweh's case the oath is to himself because there is no higher authority; he recommends Kitchen and Lawrence's treaty collection as a resource. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=6712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:52</a> | How high is 15 cubits in Genesis 7:20? | Assuming the standard 18-inch cubit, about 22.5 feet. | He notes three ancient cubit measures: the standard cubit at 18 inches, the royal cubit at 21 inches, and the divine cubit at 24 inches. Since Genesis 7:20 does not specify another measure and the standard cubit is the usual biblical default, he calculates 15 cubits as 22.5 feet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=6882" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:42</a> | What are your thoughts on the miniseries *Jesus of Nazareth*? | He does not recall seeing it; he instead comments briefly on other Bible films and series. | He says he cannot remember watching *Jesus of Nazareth*, so he cannot give a substantive judgment on that series. He instead mentions liking the long *Gospel of John* film, thinking season one of *The Chosen* was pretty good despite artistic liberties, and disliking *The Passion of the Christ* because he thought it emphasized human violence more than the wrath of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=7288" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:28</a> | As a historian, how do you react to "those who do not learn from history are doomed to repeat it"? | Knowing history does not guarantee avoiding repetition; history rhymes more than it exactly repeats. | He says people can know history and still repeat similar failures, so learning history is not a guaranteed hedge against repetition. He prefers the idea that history "rhymes" rather than repeats exactly, citing examples such as Russia and Ukraine or repeated attempts at socialism to show that similar patterns can recur even when people know the pitfalls. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=7823" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:23</a> | Do you know good scholarly works on Deuteronomy as forgery, and who was the most formidable ancient king? | He cannot recommend Deuteronomy-forgery works; he names Nebuchadnezzar II and Thutmose III as top candidates. | He says he does not know the scholarly literature on Deuteronomy-as-forgery well enough to recommend works and admits that limitation. For the ancient king question, he names Nebuchadnezzar II for defeating Assyria and Egypt and sweeping through the region, and Thutmose III for his repeated campaigns, military charisma, and love of campaign life after Hatshepsut's usurpation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=8187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:27</a> | What are your thoughts on *The Chosen*'s depiction of Moses/Moshe and its dating? | He thinks they initially got the date right but then backed away from that historical direction. | He says the series initially got the date right but then reversed course in the next episode's wilderness-serpent material, which disappointed him. In his view, they could have offered a historically and archaeologically consistent depiction of Moses, but he thinks negative reaction from parts of the evangelical and fundamentalist audience may make the show avoid firmer BC historical claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=8372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:32</a> | Would you recommend Christians read the Book of Enoch? | Only for studying Second Temple literature, not for Scripture-level guidance or new biblical insight. | He says the answer depends on why someone is reading it: it can be useful for understanding Second Temple literature, but he does not find it useful for getting more insight from the Bible itself. He warns that it is not Scripture or inspired, says new or biblically illiterate Christians should focus first on the Bible, and treats Enoch as a specialized text rather than ordinary devotional reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=8733" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:33</a> | What about believers caught up in Bible fan-fiction conspiracy theories around the Nephilim? | Some is harmless, but it can distract from gospel concerns and better interpretations. | He says a rise in Nephilim-centered fiction and conspiracy material draws partly from Michael Heiser's work, which he respects in some areas but thinks carries the Nephilim too far. He thinks some fiction is harmless, but the conspiracy-like uses are concerning because they can pull attention away from Christ, gospel essentials, and better grounded interpretations of the biblical passages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=9129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:09</a> | What is the most "metal" god other than Yahweh? | He first answers literally with Egyptian divine bodies as metal, then says for heavy-metal style one would look more to Norse gods. | He first takes "metal" literally and explains that Egyptian gods were imagined with gold flesh and iron bones, with cult images of gold or electrum functioning as the real presence of the deity. Once the question is clarified as musical heavy metal, he says that is anachronistic for the ancient Near East and that Norse gods such as Odin and Thor fit that style better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=9356" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:56</a> | Why does Moses's staff become one kind of serpent in Exodus 4 but Aaron's another before Pharaoh? | He treats the difference as significant and cautiously explores possible literary and symbolic reasons. | He says the terms are uncertain but distinguishes *nachash* as a viper from *tannin*, which is broader and closer to a serpent or dragon-like creature. Speculating cautiously, he suggests Aaron's staff may become something like a Nile monitor, because a normal snake would swallow other snakes slowly while the Pharaoh-scene devouring seems rapid; he clearly labels this as speculation needing more work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=9916" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:16</a> | Do you think Ariak king of Ellasar is Rim-Sin/Ariaku of Larsa, and why use the Sumerian name instead of Akkadian? | He has not done the reading and cannot answer yet; the row preserves that uncertainty. | He says he has looked into some related Genesis 14 figures such as the kings of Shinar and Elam, where records are better, but he has not yet done the necessary reading on Ariak, Ellasar, Rim-Sin, or Ariaku of Larsa. He treats the question as a useful prompt for future research and explicitly says he does not know yet rather than offering an unsupported identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/ravUgMK9MEU?t=10281" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:21</a> | Did the Israelites go through the wilderness in Africa? | He says no, locating the wilderness experience in Sinai rather than Africa more broadly. | He answers no and says the topography and toponyms point conclusively to the Sinai wilderness. He adds that the Sinai was more like savannah than desert at the time, and that the place names found in Egyptian records correspond with those in Exodus and Numbers. |
